body:
CONFIRMED BY RUNNING IT (2026-07-21). Not a theory - `test/gateway/membership-negation-probe.test.ts` reproduces it and is committed SKIPPED. **Un-skipping that file is step one of this ticket**; the rail already exists and does not pass.

WHAT HAPPENS. Append a claim, retract it with an ordinary negation delta, then seed a quarantine with a membership Term that selects the claim but not the negation (trivially common - the probe uses `hasPointer{context: height}`, and a negation points at a DELTA, not a context, so it is never a member). In the pool the claim reads as LIVE. The retraction did not survive the seeding edge.

WHY. rhizomatic's `negated(d, D)` ranges over the OPERAND SET (SPEC-2 §4.3): suppression is a property of the set being evaluated, not of the delta. Filter a set and keep a target while dropping its negation, and the target comes back un-suppressed. The substrate has this pinned as a conformance vector - `select-then-mask-scopes-to-operand` in `eval-basic.json`, 'the negation d4 is excluded by the select, so nothing in the subset is suppressed.' It is correct substrate behavior; Loam's seeding edge is what is wrong.

Surfaced by the rhizomatic side on issue #27, where it was raised about a FUTURE property->wall migration (§28.4). Checking whether it applied to shipped code found that it does: `QuarantineOptions.membership` (T15, [#132](https://github.com/bombadil-labs/loam/pull/132)) is a filter of exactly that shape.

WHAT IS NOT BROKEN, so the fix does not flail: §24.8 ERASURE is intact and was handled deliberately - `quarantine-pool.ts` passes TOMBSTONES across the seeding edge unconditionally (`admit: (d) => isTombstone(d.claims) || admit(d)`), so a forgotten byte is still forgotten in every pool, and `test/gateway/quarantine.test.ts` pins it byte-for-byte. The hole is ORDINARY NEGATION - retraction, revocation, any `negates` pointer that is not a tombstone. The existing code proves the shape of the fix is already understood; it was simply applied to one class of negation and not the other.

FIX (recommendation): the seeding edge must carry the NEGATION CLOSURE of whatever it admits. A delta crosses if the membership Term selects it OR it negates something that crossed - transitively, since negating a negation revives (the algebra `lawfulNegated` already walks). Content addressing keeps that chain acyclic, and `lawfulNegated` memoizes it, so the closure is cheap and the existing traversal can likely be reused rather than re-implemented.

Decide and state one thing explicitly: the closure admits deltas the membership Term did NOT select, so a pool's contents are no longer exactly `select(term)`. That is correct - a scope may narrow what you SEE but must never resurrect what was struck - but it makes membership 'the Term plus its negation closure,' and §27.6 currently says a container's members ARE the Term's dset. Amend that sentence rather than leaving the two definitions to drift.

RAILS:
- Un-skip the probe: a claim retracted in the primary must not read as live in a pool seeded by a Term that excludes its negation.
- TRANSITIVE: negation-of-a-negation revives the claim, and that revival must survive the edge too (strike, counter-strike, seed, read - the claim is live in the pool because it is live in the primary, not by accident of filtering).
- The closure does not LEAK: a negation whose target never crossed must not drag the target in. The closure follows negations of admitted deltas, not the reverse.
- Live-follow keeps it: a retraction landing in the primary AFTER seeding reaches the pool on the next pulse (the probe covers the seed path; this covers `reseed`).
- §24.8 unchanged - `test/gateway/quarantine.test.ts` stays green with no assertion softened. This ticket must not disturb the erasure path.
- `select()` ITSELF is unchanged and still returns exactly the Term's dset. The closure belongs to the SEEDING EDGE, not to the reading surface - a caller asking 'what does this Term select' must still get that answer.

Also check, before assuming the blast radius is one function: every other place Loam filters a delta-set into a narrower one. `offeredDeltas`/`offeredLens` is the obvious sibling - a federation peer pulling through an offered lens could receive a claim without its retraction, which would be the same bug pointed outward at a stranger's store. If it is, this ticket covers both and says so.

THIRD SITE, FOUND IN THE FALLOUT SWEEP (2026-07-21): **`Gateway.freeze` has the same shape** - `freezeMembers(selectImpl(this, term))` captures exactly the Term's dset with no closure (src/gateway/gateway.ts, container-identity.ts; T29, landed the same day). LATENT rather than live, because nothing consumes a ModuleVersion yet - but a module version exists precisely to be SHIPPED and loaded elsewhere, so it becomes a real bug the moment T30 or T32 builds on it, and it is cheaper to fix while nothing depends on the addresses.

It needs a DECISION rather than a silent patch, because the closure changes what `freeze` returns and therefore the content address: T29's rail asserting exact membership (`test/gateway/container-identity.test.ts`) would have to change with it. Recommendation: YES, carry the closure - a version that ships a claim without its retraction hands its consumer a withdrawn claim reading as live, which is the whole failure this ticket is about. Note that the address consequence is CORRECT, not a wart: two stores freezing the same Term where one holds a retraction and one does not get different addresses, because they are genuinely different sets, and the address is doing its job by saying so. Update the T29 rails to expect the closure rather than working around it.

edges.contract: T32 lifts the quarantine pool into the general Container, and every container is seeded through a membership filter. Landing the lifting before this fix generalizes the hole to every posture instead of one - and §28's property->wall path has the same shape, so the negation-closure rule wants to exist before more code depends on the filter.

title: A membership-scoped seeding edge drops the negation closure - retracted claims come back alive in the pool
